questions.question: Which combinator selects all descendants at any nesting level?
questions.explanation: The descendant combinator (a space) selects all elements that are descendants of the specified ancestor, at any depth.

questions.question: What does the child combinator (>) do?
questions.explanation: The child combinator (>) selects elements that are DIRECT children of the specified parent, not nested descendants.

questions.question: What is the difference between + and ~ combinators?
options: + selects immediate sibling, ~ selects all following siblings
+ works with classes, ~ works with IDs
questions.explanation: The adjacent sibling combinator (+) selects the immediately following sibling, while the general sibling combinator (~) selects all following siblings.

questions.question: Given the selector 'div > p', what happens to a p inside a div that is inside another div?
options: It's NOT selected (not a direct child)
questions.explanation: The child combinator only selects direct children. A p nested deeper (div > div > p) is not a direct child and won't be selected.

questions.question: What does the selector 'h2 + p' target?
options: Only the first p after each h2
questions.explanation: The adjacent sibling combinator (+) selects only the immediately following sibling. Only the first p after an h2 is selected.

questions.question: How do browsers read CSS selectors?
questions.explanation: Browsers read selectors right-to-left (inside-out). They first find the target element, then check if it matches the ancestor/sibling conditions.

questions.question: What is the specificity of 'div > p.highlight'?
questions.explanation: The selector has one class (highlight) = 0,1,0 and two elements (div, p) = 0,0,2, totaling 0,1,1.

questions.question: Which combinator would you use to select all li elements that are direct children of a ul?
questions.explanation: ul > li uses the child combinator to select only li elements that are direct children of a ul, not nested within other lists.

questions.question: What does the selector 'p ~ span' select?
options: All spans that are siblings after any p
questions.explanation: The general sibling combinator (~) selects all spans that are siblings of and appear after any p in the same parent.

questions.question: Can combinators be chained together?
options: Only space and > can be chained
questions.explanation: Combinators can be chained to create precise selection paths, e.g., div > ul > li selects li that are direct children of ul that are direct children of div.

questions.question: What is the difference between '.card .title' and '.card > .title'?
options: First selects any descendant, second only direct child
First selects direct child, second any descendant
questions.explanation: .card .title selects ANY .title inside .card (any depth), while .card > .title selects only direct child .title elements.

questions.question: Which selector would target a paragraph that comes immediately after an h3 inside an article?
questions.explanation: Both 'article h3 + p' and 'article > h3 + p' would work. The first uses descendant, the second uses child, but both reach h3 and then use adjacent sibling.

questions.question: What happens if no element matches a combinator selector?
questions.explanation: If no element matches the selector, the styles are simply not applied. No error occurs.

questions.question: Which is a performance concern with combinators?
options: Deep descendant selectors can be slower
questions.explanation: Very deep descendant selectors (e.g., div ul li a span) can be slower because the browser checks many elements right-to-left. Keep selectors shallow.

questions.question: What is the recommended maximum depth for CSS selectors?
questions.explanation: Best practice recommends keeping selector depth to 3 levels or fewer for readability and performance.

questions.question: The selector 'div p' uses which combinator?
questions.explanation: The space between div and p is the descendant combinator, selecting all p elements that are descendants of a div.

questions.question: Can you use combinators with pseudo-classes?
questions.explanation: Combinators can be combined with pseudo-classes to create more specific selections, like ul > li:first-child.

questions.question: What does the selector 'nav a' select?
options: Only direct children a of nav
All a elements inside nav at any depth
All nav elements that have a children
Only the first a inside nav
questions.explanation: The descendant combinator (space) selects all a elements that are descendants of nav, regardless of nesting depth.

questions.question: What is an advantage of using combinators over adding more classes?
questions.explanation: Combinators allow you to target elements based on their position in the DOM, reducing the need for extra classes in the HTML.

questions.question: Which combinator would you use to style a paragraph that follows an h2, but NOT paragraphs that come before it?
options: Both A and B would work
questions.explanation: Both h2 + p (adjacent) and h2 ~ p (general sibling) select paragraphs that come AFTER h2. Neither selects paragraphs before h2.
